coredata.dc:title: Cobalt/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanosheets from coal-based graphene quantum dots boosted oxygen reduction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qin
Lei, Jing
Zhao, Xueyang
Geng, Qin
Xie, Hongtao
Li, Yizhao
Cao, Yali
coredata.dc:description: We report on cobalt/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanosheets (Co/NCNs) as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The Co/NCNs were synthesized through pyrolysis at 900 °C using coal-based graphene quantum dots that contain nitrogen as a source of both carbon and nitrogen, NaCl as a template, and trace amounts of cobalt salt as a metal source. The Co/NCNs-0.1 exhibited ORR activity that was equivalent to that of Pt/C, with a half-wave potential of 0.85 V and a limiting current density of 4.9 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the zinc-air battery incorporating Co/NCNs-0.1 demonstrated an open circuit voltage of 1.423 V, a maximum power density of 149 mW cm−2, and a specific capacity of 806.5 mAh g−1 at a current density of 20 mA cm−2. The results of density functional theory calculations indicate that metal Co, when protected by graphitic-N-doped carbon, holds a greater negative charge and exhibits a lower energy barrier compared to other types of nitrogen species. This property contributes to the catalytic activity of the ORR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cobalt/nitrogen doped carbon nanosheets
Graphene quantum dots
Oxygen reduction reaction
Zinc-air battery

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of the Co/NCNs.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra and (c) SEM images of NCNs-0.1, Co/NCNs-0.2, Co/NCNs-0.1 and Co/NCNs-0.05; (d) EDS elemental mapping of Co/NCNs-0.1; (e) TEM and HRTEM images of NCNs-0.1 and Co/NCNs-0.1; (f) Co 2p spectra, (g) N 1 s spectra, (h) the contents of different nitrogen species, (i) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of Co/NCNs-0.05, Co/NCNs-0.1, Co/NCNs-0.2 and NCNs-0.1.

(a) CV curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 in N2– and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a scan rate of 100 mV/s; (b) LSV curves, (c) Tafel curves, (d) the column chart of E1/2 and j of NCNs-0.1, Co/NCNs-0.2, Co/NCNs-0.1 and Co/NCNs-0.05 and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV/s with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm; (e) LSV curves and K-L curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV/s with different rotating speeds; (f) LSV curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm by RRDE; (g) Electron transfer number n and H2O2 yield; (h) I-t curves obtained by chronoamperometry in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH; (i) Methanol tolerance curves with the addition of 1 M methanol.

(a, b) Free energy diagrams of ORR (U=1.23 V); (c) Total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS); (d) Differential charge density of Co-C-gN (the colors yellow and blue represent the accumulation and depletion of charge density, respectively).

(a) Open-circuit potential test of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C; (b) Polarization and power density curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C; (c) Specific capacity curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C; (d) CVs on the Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C after 15 cycles and 1000 cycles in O2-saturated 0.1 mol/L KOH solution (sweep rate: 10 mV s−1).

head.ce:title: Cobalt/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanosheets from coal-based graphene quantum dots boosted oxygen reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Institute of Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources
Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials
Autonomous Region; Institute of Applied Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Autonomous Region; Institute of Applied Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Smart and Clean Energy, Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Smart and Clean Energy
University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Smart and Clean Energy, Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou), University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Huzhou 313001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cobalt/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanosheets are developed as an efficient electrocatalyst for the four-electron oxygen reduction reaction (4e-ORR). Metal Co protected by graphitic-N-doped carbon holds more negative charge and has a lower energy barrier than that of other types of N species, which contributes to the high activity.

We report on cobalt/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanosheets (Co/NCNs) as a highly efficient electrocatalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The Co/NCNs were synthesized through pyrolysis at 900 °C using coal-based graphene quantum dots that contain nitrogen as a source of both carbon and nitrogen, NaCl as a template, and trace amounts of cobalt salt as a metal source. The Co/NCNs-0.1 exhibited ORR activity that was equivalent to that of Pt/C, with a half-wave potential of 0.85 V and a limiting current density of 4.9 mA cm−2. Furthermore, the zinc-air battery incorporating Co/NCNs-0.1 demonstrated an open circuit voltage of 1.423 V, a maximum power density of 149 mW cm−2, and a specific capacity of 806.5 mAh g−1 at a current density of 20 mA cm−2. The results of density functional theory calculations indicate that metal Co, when protected by graphitic-N-doped carbon, holds a greater negative charge and exhibits a lower energy barrier compared to other types of nitrogen species. This property contributes to the catalytic activity of the ORR.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc-air batteries are widely regarded as the optimal energy source for electric vehicles due to their high energy density and high specific capacity. However, the performance of rechargeable zinc-air batteries is significantly limited by the inefficient oxygen reaction kinetics at the air cathode and poor cycling stability caused by the anode's dissipation and deformation in conventional alkaline electrolytes[1,2]. These factors have impeded the commercialization of zinc-air batteries. The oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is crucial in energy conversion and storage devices, such as fuel cells and metal-air batteries[3–5]. However, due to its multi-electron nature and involvement of multiple reaction intermediates, ORR has a slow reaction rate[6–9]. Therefore, it is of utmost importance to develop efficient electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction that exhibit high selectivity and stability. Currently, three types of catalysts have been extensively researched: precious metal catalysts, carbon-based catalysts, and transition metal-based catalysts[10–12]. Among these, noble metal Pt-based catalysts are considered to be the most efficient catalysts for the four-electron ORR (4e-ORR). However, their high cost and poor stability have hindered their widespread commercial application. Hence, it is crucial and pressing to create and advance a range of non-noble metal electrocatalysts that are both low-cost and highly active, in order to overcome the constraints imposed by the scarcity of noble metal resources. Similar to the d orbital vacancy found in Pt-based catalysts, which is associated with their high activity, non-noble metal catalysts based on transition metals that also exhibit d orbital vacancy are being extensively researched[13–15].

Cobalt is one of the abundant elements on earth and has a metal redox potential that is close to the potential in oxygen reduction to water (E=1.23 V vs. RHE) and with various oxidation states. Cobalt-based catalysts have been widely recognized as potential candidate materials due to their comparable or even superior activity compared to noble metals. To expose more active sites, minimizing the size of transition metal-based electrocatalysts is an effective strategy to increase their active site utilization[16]. However, reducing the catalyst size to the nanoscale often leads to severe agglomeration[17,18]. To address this issue, a suitable support material can be selected to anchor the nanoscale Co-based catalysts and improve the dispersion of their active sites. This approach can significantly enhance the electrocatalytic activity, durability, and tolerance to methanol, such as carbon. Wang et al.[19] prepared cobalt nanoparticle-encapsulated carbon fibers using electrostatic spinning synthesis and pyrolysis strategies. The optimized catalyst, containing both Co-Ox and Co-Ny active sites, has an ORR half-wave potential of 0.85 V, which demonstrated an excellent ORR catalytic performance. Co-carbon matrix composites (Co-C) can modify the atomic coordination environment and electronic structure, thereby enhancing their catalytic activity[20].

Although Co-C type catalysts exhibit high catalytic activity in ORR, they also have some drawbacks, such as the trade-off between long-term stability and high catalytic activity. The active sites may be more vulnerable to complex catalytic reaction environments, which can result in catalyst deactivation due to the reconfiguration of the catalyst surface and the disruption of its geometrical or electronic structure[21]. On the other hand, the introduction of nitrogen into Co-C materials enhances the electrical conductivity of the carbon matrix and increases the number of active sites. Additionally, it can further modify the density of states near the Fermi level, thereby impacting its catalytic activity[22–25]. Furthermore, the nitrogen-doped carbon layer can protect the metal nanoparticles from agglomeration and oxidation during catalysis, which in turn enhances the structural stability and durability of the catalyst[26]. In certain instances, the efficiency of electron transfer and reaction kinetics of the catalyst may be improved through the formation of Co-N bonds. Xu et al.[27] have indicated that the Co-N structure present in the Co-N-C material results in a high equilibrium potential of Co3+/Co2+, which is less prone to Fenton-like reactions, and exhibits exceptional performance in 4e-ORR. The introduction of Co-N bonds also facilitates the generation of Co-N active sites, which exhibit exceptional catalytic activity towards ORR. Deng et al.[28] systematically designed and investigated a theoretical model for Co-Nx (x = 2 and 4) and discovered that the Co-N2 model can effectively refine the rate-determining steps of the ORR. However, the carbon and nitrogen sources used in the synthesis of Co-C-N materials reported in existing works are typically derived from various precursors containing carbon and nitrogen. The synthesis process of the catalyst is complex and involves multiple high-temperature carbonization or pyrolysis steps, which may suffer from severe aggregation of active sites, low specific surface area, and deterioration of pore structure, thus limiting the activity of the catalysts[29]. N-doped coal-based graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) show significant potential in ORR owing to their distinctive electronic structure and exceptional electrocatalytic properties. The utilization of N-GQDs can serve as a dual carbon and nitrogen source, thereby streamlining the multi-step synthesis of Co-C-N materials and simplifying the preparation process, thus reducing the production cost and time[30].

In Co-C-N materials, variations in the figure of merit among different nitrogen types (e.g., graphite N (gN), pyridine N (pdN), and pyrrole N (prN)) can exert a substantial influence on the surface electronic structure of catalysts, thereby affecting their catalytic performance in ORR[31,32]. In general, gN can optimize the electronic structure of Co by providing additional electrons, thus increasing its capacity to bind with oxygen molecules[33]; pdN, due to its higher electronegativity, is able to enhance the electrophilicity of Co sites, which helps to lower the activation energy barrier of oxygen molecules[34]; and prN, although it may exhibit lower intrinsic activity in some cases, its activity can be obtained by interacting with adjacent pyridinic nitrogen sites to significantly enhanced[35]. Therefore, it is imperative to rationally design transition metal–carbon materials that possess ample nitrogen doping sites and discern their active sites and mechanisms during the process of ORR. In this work, N-GQDs were utilized as both carbon and nitrogen sources, for the rich N species and edge-rich defects accelerated the formation of metal-N (M−N) sites[24,36,37]. NaCl was used as a template to facilitate the self-assembly of N-GQDs to form porous nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheets with abundant defects. Highly dispersed cobalt/nitrogen co-doped carbon nanosheets (Co/NCNs) were synthesized by incorporating cobalt salts to improve oxygen reduction performance. The interaction force between Co and N atoms resulted in the formation of a Co-N bond, which accelerated the electron transport and facilitated the 4e-ORR process. The optimized catalysts exhibited comparable ORR catalytic activities to Pt/C and demonstrated superior stability compared to Pt/C, resulting in superior performance of the assembled zinc-air battery. Further density functional theory (DFT) calculations illustrate that the graphitized N adjusts the charge density of adjacent carbon atoms and coordinates with Co to form Co-N bonds. This raises the Fermi level, which facilitates the electron transfer from the Co center to the reactants, lowering the free energy of intermediate adsorption, and accelerating the onset of 4e-ORR.

Preparation of N-GQDs: Coal-based GQDs were prepared by a modified chemical oxidation method as described in the literature. 20 mL of mixed acid (VHNO3: VH 2 SO 4 = 3:1, HNO3 and H2SO4 are 63 % and 98 % acids, respectively) was slowly added to 1 g of fine bituminous coals and refluxed at 80 °C for 10 h. Then it was separated by centrifugation and washed with a large water. Finally, the N-GQDs were prepared by dialysis.

Preparation of Co/NCNs: 0.1 g of the prepared N-GQDs was weighed and dispersed evenly into 20 mL of distilled water. 10 mg of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 5 mL of distilled water to form a solution, which was added to the dispersed solution of the N-GQDs slowly and dropwise under the condition of stirring. 1 g of NaCl was dissolved in 5 mL of distilled water, quickly poured into the above solution, and stirred for 1 h. The brown precursor was obtained after freeze-drying for 12 h. Then, it was put into a corundum boat in a tube furnace under the atmospheric protection of Ar and was heated up to 900 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 and held for 1 h to obtain the target product. Finally, the product was washed with a large amount of distilled water to remove the NaCl. The black solid product obtained by filtration was dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water and freeze-dried for 12 h, which was denoted as Co/NCNs-0.1. NCNs-0.1 were obtained while keeping all the steps unchanged and without Co(NO3)2·6H2O.

Preparation of Co/NCNs-x: Only changing the ratio of N-GQDs: NaCl (mN-
GQDs: m NaCl = 0.2: 1 and 0.05: 1) while keeping all the steps unchanged after fixing the mass ratio of Co(NO3)2·6H2O: N-GQDs to 0.1: 1, which was denoted as Co/NCNs-0.2 and Co/NCNs-0.05.

Preparation of Co/CNs-x: To explore the effect of the template (NaCl), changing the ratio of Co(NO3)2·6H2O: N-GQDs (m Co(NO3)2·6H2O: mN-
GQDs = 0.05: 1, 0.1: 1 and 0.2: 1) without NaCl while keeping all the steps unchanged, which was denoted as Co/CNs-0.1, Co/CNs-0.2 and Co/CNs-0.05.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800H), transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi-600), and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM-2010F) were used to investigate the morphology and structure of samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) was carried out at 10-80°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected with an ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer.

5 mg samples were dispersed in a mixed solution of water (300 mL), ethanol (680 mL), and Nafion (20 mL, 5 wt%) to form an ink. The working electrodes were prepared by dispersing 10 mL ink onto the rotating disk electrode (RDE). The electrochemical ORR tests (cyclic voltammetry (CV), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)) were carried out on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation in 0.1 M KOH solution

Zinc-air battery test: 6 mg sample was dispersed in water (490 mL), ethanol (490 mL), and Nafion (20 mL, 5 wt%) to form an ink, and ultrasonicated for about 1 h. The dispersing solution was spread onto the carbon paper with a mass loading of about 1 mg cm−2 and then dried naturally as the cathode, and a 0.5 mm zinc plate as the anode to assemble the zinc-air battery. The battery performance was tested in 6 M KOH electrolyte.

The preparation process of the samples is shown in Fig. 1 , and to further optimize the catalyst, the initial investigation focuses on the influence of the catalyst structure based on the ratio of metal salts to N-GQDs. The coal-based N-GQDs with a diameter of approximately 5 nm were prepared by a modified chemical oxidation method (Fig. S1). The template ratio has a significant impact on the morphology and performance of Co/NCNs-x. When no template agent is added, the samples exhibit a thickly layered structure with a rough surface and numerous Co particles with mediocre performances (Fig. S2). Fig. 2 a shows XRD patterns to analyze the phase composition of NCNs-0.1 and the Co/NCNs-x samples with template NaCl, where all samples exhibit an amorphous carbon peak near 22.0°. However, no peak related to Co was observed, possibly due to the relatively low amount of cobalt introduced. With the assistance of the template, cobalt nanoparticles could disperse more evenly on the carbon sheets, leading to the absence of a Co phase. The Raman spectra (Fig. 2b) show that the intensity ratio of D and G peaks (ID/IG ) values for NCNs-0.1, Co/NCNs-0.2, Co/NCNs-0.1, and Co/NCNs-0.05 are 0.97, 1.00, 1.01, and 0.99, respectively. Peak D is usually related to the degree of defects and disorder in carbon materials, the G-peak is usually related to the sp2 hybrid in-plane stretching vibration of carbon atoms, indicating the lattice vibration and order degree of the material. Therefore, the higher the ID/IG value, the higher the degree of defects and disorder in the material[38,39]. This indicates that the introduction of the NaCl template can increase the defect density of the samples. Co/NCNs-0.1 has the highest ID/IG value, that is, the highest defect density, which may be an important factor for its high ORR catalytic activity.

The SEM images of NCNs-0.1 and Co/NCNs-x were displayed in Fig. 2c. The NCNs-0.1 without metal exhibit a disordered nanosheet structure. When increasing the mass of template NaCl, Co/NCNs-x did not significantly change the morphology. The EDS elemental mappings of Co/NCNs-0.1 (Fig. 2d) and other samples show that Co, C, N, and O elements are uniformly distributed on the surface of the material (Fig. S3), and the content of Co is less, which is consistent with the analysis of XRD results. TEM images of NCNs-0.1 and Co/NCNs-0.1 (Fig. 2e) further confirmed the formation of nanosheets due to the use of the template. The nanosheet morphology did not change before and after the introduction of metal. The HR-TEM image of Co/NCNs-0.1 clearly shows lattice fringes of Co (111) planes (d = 0.201 nm), further confirming the presence of Co nanoparticles distributed in the carbon nanosheet structure.

XPS spectra are used to analyze the surface chemical composition of samples (Fig. 2f-h, Fig. S4). The Co 2p spectra (Fig. 2f) showed that the peaks at 779.9 eV and 795.7 eV corresponded to metallic Co, 797.5 eV and 781.7 eV corresponding to Co-O, and 786.9 eV and 802.7 eV corresponding to satellite peak, respectively[40–43]. Notably, the peak at 784.4 eV corresponds to Co-N, indicating the formation of Co-N active sites[44]. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2g) show that the existence of pyridinic N (398.1 eV), Co-N (399.7 eV), pyrrolic N (400.6 eV), and graphitic N (401.6 eV) species, also confirming the formation of Co-N bonds[45,46]. These Co-N sites are important active sites in the ORR process and can effectively improve the rate-determining step of ORR. Significantly, the Co-N content in Co/NCNs-0.1 was the highest, indicating the strongest ORR activity. Studies have shown that gN and pdN are the active sites of ORR, but there are differences in their catalytic capacity[33]. Therefore, the content of these four types of N (Fig. 2h) will affect ORR activity. The C1s spectra (Fig. S4a), deconvoluted into three peaks as C–C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-N (285.3 eV) and C-O (287.8 eV), showing a high level of C, which serves as active sites for ORR[38,46]. From the O 1 s spectra (Fig. S4b), it can be seen that oxygen species is mainly composed of Co-O (530.9 eV), C-O (532.1 eV), and –COO (533.0 eV) [47]. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves are displayed in Fig. 2i and the corresponding data are listed in Table S1. The specific surface area of NCNs-0.1 is 237 m2 g−1. The introduction of a small amount of Co increases the specific surface area of the samples (Co/NCNs-0.2 (280 m2 g−1), Co/NCNs-0.1 (273 m2 g−1), Co/NCNs-0.05 (260 m2 g−1)), but it does not significantly affect the pore size distribution, which remains predominantly in the range of 3–5 nm for mesopores.

The ORR performance was conducted in a 0.1 M KOH solution. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the CV curve of Co/NCNs-0.1 under N2 saturation conditions did not show any peak, while a clear redox peak appeared under O2 saturation conditions, indicating the ORR activity of Co/NCNs-0.1. The ORR activity of the catalyst was further evaluated through the corresponding LSV curves (Fig. 3b). In order to further study the effect of surface area on the electrochemical performance, the samples with different Co contents were tested by CV in 0.1 M KOH solution at scan rates of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 mV S-1. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was estimated by the value of double-layer capacitance (Cdl ). The CV curves for each sample at different scanning rates are shown in Fig. S5. A slope plot was made to calculate Cdl based on the relationship between sweep speed and limiting current density. Cdl of NCNs-0.1, Co/NCNs-0.2, Co/NCNs-0.1, Co/NCNs-0.05, and Pt/C were 1.00, 4.96, 34.50, 33.22, and 13.75 mF cm−2, respectively. The Cdl value of Co/NCNs-0.1 was the largest, indicating that Co/NCNs-0.1 had a higher ECSA and more electrochemically active sites were exposed. The Tafel plot and column chart of half-wave potential as well as limit current density were shown in Fig. 3c-d. The non-metal NCNs-0.1 exhibited the poorest ORR activity, with a half-wave potential of 0.76 V. Compared to Co/NCNs-0.2 and Co/NCNs-0.05, Co/NCNs-0.1 demonstrated superior ORR activity (Co/NCNs-0.1 (E 1/2 = 0.85 V, j = 4.9 mA cm−2) > Co/NCNs-0.05 (E 1/2 = 0.83 V, j = 5.1 mA cm−2) > Co/NCNs-0.2 (E 1/2 = 0.80 V, j = 5.0 mA cm−2)), showing catalytic activity comparable to that of the commercial catalyst Pt/C (E 1/2 = 0.85 V, j = 5.0 mA cm−2). The Tafel slope of Co/NCNs-0.1 was 85 mV dec-1, closest to the 82 mV dec-1 of Pt/C, indicating a good kinetic reaction rate close to that of Pt/C.

The LSV curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 were tested at 400, 625, 900, 1225, 1600, and 2025 rpm (Fig. 3e). The Koutecky-Levich (K-L) curve of Co/NCNs-0.1 exhibited a good linear correlation at the potential range of 0.40–0.70 V (the inset of Fig. 3e). The average electron transfer number of the Co/NCNs-0.1 at 0.40–0.70 V was calculated as about 3.67 using the K-L curve. Additionally, LSV curves (Fig. 3f-g) were tested at 1600 rpm using a rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH, and the electron transfer number and H2O2 selectivity were calculated as 3.80 and 10.08 % for Co/NCNs-0.1, respectively, suggesting Co/NCNs-0.1 tend to favor 4e-ORR process. Stability and methanol tolerance are important indicators for evaluating catalyst performance[48]. The stability of Co/NCNs-0.1 (Fig. 3h) was tested for 5 h at 0.50 V, which showed that Co/NCNs-0.1 exhibited excellent stability, with a current retention rate of 96.8 % after 5 h, outperforming Pt/C (current retention rate of 73 %). As shown in Fig. 3i, when 1.0 M methanol was added to the electrolyte, the methanol tolerance of Co/NCNs-0.1 was significantly better than that of Pt/C. The current retention rate of Co/NCNs-0.1 was 90 % after 1 h, while the current retention of Pt/C rate was only 75 %.

To better understand the effect of different nitrogen species, DFT calculations were used to reveal the ORR mechanism on the surface of Co/NCNs. The catalytic activity of the nitrogen-doped carbon sites of the catalyst was explored by simulating the change of Gibbs free energy (ΔG) of the reaction intermediates. Based on the experimental results, the model of Co/NCNs was constructed and optimized (Fig. S6). The magnitude of adsorption energies of the OOH*, OH* and O* intermediates on different active sites of NCNs and Co/NCNs, respectively, were calculated. The active sites were C, C-gN, C-pdN, C-prN, Co-C, Co-C-gN, Co-C-pdN and Co-C-prN. At U=1.23 V, it can be seen from the oxygen adsorption free-energy step diagrams of each configuration (Fig. 4 a) and the free-energy difference of each radical reaction (Table S2) that the rate-limiting step of ORR on NCNs lies in the first step (i.e., O2 → OOH*). Among them, the energy barrier on the C-gN site was 0.71 eV, which was far lower than that of C (1.72 eV), C-pdN (1.29 eV), and C-prN (1.10 eV), suggesting that the ORR process is more likely to be carried out on the C-gN site. In contrast, the energy barriers of the O2 → OOH* steps at different sites in Co/NCNs were reduced (Fig. 4b), among which the energy barriers of Co-C (−1.67 eV) and Co-C-gN (−1.62 eV) sites were reduced most significantly. This suggests that the Co-N site formed by the introduction of Co effectively regulates the electronic structure of the active site and optimizes the ΔG of the OOH* intermediate, thus accelerating the ORR kinetic process[49]. Notably, the OOH*→O* step of the Co-C-gN (2.53 eV) site requires less increased energy compared to Co-C (3.43 eV), suggesting that the Co-C-gN structure is the most 4e-ORR active site in the material and that the interaction of Co with graphitic N of Co/NCNs to promote the ORR kinetics[31].

Fig. 4c shows the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) at different sites in the four configurations of Co/NCNs. The calculated Fermi level in Co-C-gN was −1.17 eV, which was higher than that of Co-C (−1.21 eV), Co-C-pdN (−1.47 eV), and Co-C-prN (−1.58 eV). It is shown that the interaction of graphitized N with Co led to the elevation of the Fermi level, which implied a downward shift of the d-band center of metallic Co[50–53]. This may promote the electron transfer from the metal center to the reactants and the reduction of the free energy of intermediate adsorption, contributing to the improvement of the ORR activity of Co/NCNs[52,54]. In order to further investigate the electronic structure of Co/NCNs, differential charge density analysis was performed (Fig. 4d). It can be seen that cobalt as the metal center loses electrons and exhibits oxidative properties, while oxygen atoms gain electrons and exhibit reductive properties during the reaction. The coordination of Co with graphite N facilitates the rapid transfer of electrons, which further reduces the adsorption energy of OOH* intermediates and accelerates the reaction, ensuring the high catalytic activity of Co/NCNs for ORR[46]. In summary, the Co-C-gN structure is the site with the highest 4e-ORR activity in Co/NCNs, and the graphitized N is the key that can promote the ORR kinetics of Co/NCNs[22], which is consistent with the experimental results that Co/NCNs exhibits high 4e-ORR performance.

In order to showcase the practicality of the material, the Co/NCNs-0.1 catalyst and a commercial Pt/C catalyst were assembled into a zinc-air battery, and the test results are shown in Fig. 5 . The open-circuit voltages of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C are 1.425 V and 1.454 V, respectively, and the voltages are maintained at 1.423 V and 1.467 V, respectively, after 900 s of continuous testing, in contrast to Co/NCNs-0.1, which maintains a more stable voltage (Fig. 5a). From the polarisation and power density curves (Fig. 5b), the power density of Co/NCNs-0.1 is 149 mW cm−2, which is better than that of Pt/C (82 mW cm−2). After normalizing for Zn consumption (Fig. 5c), the specific capacities of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C were determined to be 806.5 mAh g−1 and 756.3 mAh g−1, respectively. Fig. 5d shows the comparison of the CV curves of Co/NCNs-0.1 and Pt/C after 15 circles of scanning in an O2-saturated 0.1 mol L-1 KOH solution and after 1000 circles of continuous scanning, respectively. As shown in the figure, the peak potential of the Co/NCNs-0.1 catalyst after 1000 circles of cycling is comparable to that at the initial stage, indicating the excellent stability of the catalyst. Compared with the recently reported Co-based catalysts (Tables S3–S4) [55–61], Co/NCNs-0.1 also demonstrated exceptional ORR and zinc-air battery performance.

In summary, the preparation of porous Co/NCNs-x was prepared from multi-functional N-GQD by a simple solution method using NaCl as a template is reported. Co/NCNs-0.1 demonstrates ORR activity that is comparable to Pt/C, with a half-wave potential of 0.85 V, a limiting current density of 4.9 mA cm−2, excellent stability, and methanol tolerance. Furthermore, a primary zinc-air battery assembled with Co/NCNs-0.1 achieves an open-circuit voltage of 1.423 V, a maximum power density of 149 mW cm−2, and a specific capacity of 806.5 mAh g−1 at a current density of 20 mA cm−2. DFT analysis revealed that the active site of Co with graphitic N in Co/NCNs possesses the lowest adsorption energy for OOH*, facilitates charge transfer during the electrocatalytic process, and significantly enhances the performance of ORR. This study aims to improve the comprehension of ORR mechanisms in transition metal/N-doped carbon materials and provide guidance for the development of highly efficient non-precious metal electrocatalysts for renewable energy conversions. The material also has potential applications in imaging, sensing, photocatalysis, solar cells and many other fields, which could help open up new markets and application areas.

Qin Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Jing Lei: Methodology, Data curation. Xueyang Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Qin Geng: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Hongtao Xie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Yizhao Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Yali Cao: Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52100166, 22202032, and 22306023) and the Research Initiation Project of Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou) of UESTC (No. U03210037).
